subject: ela
title: Figurative Language: Similes and Metaphors
content:
Figurative Language

What is Figurative Language?

Figurative language is when writers use words in creative, non-literal ways to create images and emotions. It's not meant to be taken literally — it's meant to paint a picture in your mind!

Similes

A simile compares two things using like or as.
• "Her smile was like sunshine" (comparing smile to sunshine)
• "He ran as fast as a cheetah" (comparing speed to a cheetah)

Similes always use like or as to make the comparison.

Metaphors

A metaphor compares two things WITHOUT using like or as. It states one thing IS another.
• "Her smile was sunshine" (her smile IS sunshine)
• "Life is a journey" (life IS a journey)

Metaphors are stronger than similes because they say the comparison is true.

Why Use Them?

• Create vivid images in readers' minds
• Help readers understand feelings
• Make writing more interesting and memorable
• Express emotions in new ways

Practice Identifying

1. "The world is my oyster" → Metaphor (世界=oyster in a comparing way, no like/as)
2. "Her voice was like music" → Simile (uses like)
3. "Time is a thief" → Metaphor (time literally is a thief)
4. "The snow was like a white blanket" → Simile (uses like)

questions.prompt: "He eats like a pig" is an example of what?
"Life is a roller coaster" is what type of figurative language?
What makes a metaphor different from a simile?
"The stars are diamond scattered across the night sky" is an example of what?
questions.hint: Stars ARE diamonds without using like or as

questions.prompt: Why do writers use figurative language?
choices: To create vivid images and emotions
questions.hint: Think about the effect on readers
